cards.heading: Prehistoric India — Stone Age
points: Palaeolithic Age (Old Stone Age): 500,000 – 10,000 BCE; tools: hand axe, cleaver, scraper; sites: Bhimbetka (MP), Hunsgi (Karnataka), Attirampakkam (Tamil Nadu)
Mesolithic Age: 10,000 – 6000 BCE; microliths (small blades); rock paintings at Bhimbetka
Neolithic Age: 6000 – 1000 BCE; agriculture begins, polished stone tools, pottery; sites: Mehrgarh (Balochistan — earliest Neolithic site in Indian subcontinent)
Chalcolithic Age: Use of copper alongside stone; sites: Jorwe (Maharashtra), Ahar (Rajasthan), Malwa
Iron Age: 1500 – 600 BCE; iron tools changed agriculture and warfare; associated with Vedic civilization
cards.alert: ⭐ Bhimbetka (MP) — UNESCO World Heritage Site; has rock shelters showing human occupation from Lower Palaeolithic to Medieval period. Mehrgarh is earliest evidence of farming in the subcontinent.

cards.heading: Indus Valley / Harappan Civilization (2600–1900 BCE)
points: Discovery: Harappa excavated by Daya Ram Sahni (1921); Mohenjo-daro by R.D. Banerji (1922)
Extent: 1.5 million sq km — largest Bronze Age civilization; spread across Punjab, Sindh, Rajasthan, Gujarat, UP, Maharashtra
Major sites in India: Kalibangan (Rajasthan), Lothal (Gujarat), Dholavira (Gujarat), Rakhigarhi (Haryana — largest Indian site), Banawali (Haryana), Surkotada (Gujarat)
Dholavira: Had a unique water reservoir system and stadium-like structure; inscription board
Rakhigarhi: Largest Harappan site in India; DNA analysis showed no Steppe ancestry
Lothal: Dockyard (first in world); bead factory; double burial; evidence of rice cultivation

rows: Grid pattern; streets at right angles; burnt brick construction
Underground covered drains — most advanced in ancient world
Mohenjo-daro; 12m×7m; ritual bathing; waterproofed with bitumen
Harappa & Mohenjo-daro; largest structures; evidence of surplus storage
Pictographic (400+ signs); boustrophedon (right to left); NOT deciphered yet
Mother Goddess; Pashupati seal; fire altars at Kalibangan; no temples found

cards.heading: Decline of Indus Valley Civilization
points: Aryan Invasion Theory (Mortimer Wheeler): now largely discredited; no archaeological evidence of battle
Climate Change Theory: Aridification; monsoon shift; dried up of Saraswati/Ghaggar-Hakra river
Flood Theory: Repeated floods changed river course at Mohenjo-daro
Epidemic Theory: Evidence of unburied bodies at Mohenjo-daro
Trade Decline: Collapse of trade networks with Mesopotamia around 2000 BCE
Most accepted today: Multi-causal — climate change + floods + disease + internal breakdown
cards.alert: ⭐ Saraswati River (now Ghaggar-Hakra) — satellite images show ancient river bed; most Harappan sites (over 500) are along this river, not Indus. Hence some scholars prefer 'Saraswati Civilization'.

cards.heading: Vedic Period — Early Vedic (1500–1000 BCE)
points: Rigvedic geography: Sapta Sindhu (seven rivers — Indus, Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Beas, Sutlej, Saraswati)
Society: Tribal (jana); patriarchal; Varna not rigid; women had education (Gargi, Maitreyi)
Economy: Cattle-based (godhana = cow wealth); some agriculture; barter system
Polity: Raja (tribal chief); Sabha (assembly of elders); Samiti (general assembly of people)
Gods: Indra (most mentioned, 250 hymns), Agni (fire), Varuna (water/cosmic order), Soma
Rigveda: 1028 hymns; 10 mandalas; oldest book of the world still in use; UNESCO inscription

cards.heading: Later Vedic Period (1000–600 BCE)
points: Geography expanded: Ganga-Yamuna doab became center; eastward migration
Society: Varna system became rigid (Brahmin, Kshatriya, Vaishya, Shudra); women's status declined
Economy: Agriculture dominant; iron plough used; rice became main crop
Polity: Janapadas emerged (territorial states); kingship became hereditary; Rajasuya, Ashvamedha, Vajapeya yajnas
Philosophy: Upanishads (108) emerged — Brahman-Atman, Moksha, Karma, Samsara
Later Vedic literature: Brahmanas (ritual texts), Aranyakas (forest texts), Upanishads (philosophical)
cards.alert: ⭐ Chandogya Upanishad contains 'Tat Tvam Asi' (That thou art). Brihadaranyaka Upanishad is the largest. Mundaka — 'Satyameva Jayate' (Truth alone triumphs) — India's national motto.

cards.heading: Mahajanapadas (600–300 BCE)
points: 16 Mahajanapadas mentioned in Anguttara Nikaya (Buddhist text)
Most powerful: Magadha, Kosala, Vatsa, Avanti
Magadha rose to power due to: iron ore in Chota Nagpur, fertile Gangetic plain, strategic location, elephants
Haryanka dynasty: Bimbisara (first historical king; married into Kosala & Lichchhavi), Ajatashatru (killed Bimbisara; expanded Magadha)
Shishunaga dynasty: Defeated Avanti; moved capital to Vaishali briefly
Nanda dynasty: First non-Kshatriya dynasty; Dhana Nanda (last Nanda) — Alexander's contemporary; huge army
Republic states (Ganas/Sanghas): Vajji (Vaishali), Mallas, Shakyas (Buddha's clan), Lichchhavis

cards.heading: Rise of New Religions — Buddhism & Jainism
points: Cause: Revolt against Brahmanical domination, complex rituals, and Varna discrimination
Vardhamana Mahavira (599–527 BCE): 24th Tirthankara; born in Vaishali; attained Kevala Jnana at Jambikagrama; died at Pavapuri
Jainism: 5 Mahavratas — Ahimsa, Satya, Asteya, Brahmacharya, Aparigraha; Syadvada (relativism); Anekantavada
Jain sects: Digambara (sky-clad; no clothes) vs Shvetambara (white-clad)
Gautama Buddha (563–483 BCE): Born at Lumbini (Nepal); father Suddhodana; mother Mahamaya
Four Sights that changed Siddhartha: Old man, Sick man, Dead body, Wandering ascetic
Enlightenment (Nirvana): At Bodh Gaya under Peepal tree; First sermon at Sarnath (Dhammachakka Pavattana — Turning of Wheel of Law)
Four Noble Truths (Arya Satya): 1.Life is suffering (Dukkha) 2.Desire causes suffering (Samudaya) 3.Cessation possible (Nirodha) 4.Eightfold Path leads to cessation (Magga)
cards.alert: ⭐ Eightfold Path: Right View, Right Thought, Right Speech, Right Action, Right Livelihood, Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, Right Concentration. Three Jewels of Buddhism: Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha. Buddha died at Kushinagar (Mahaparinirvana).

cards.heading: Maurya Empire (321–185 BCE)
points: Founded by Chandragupta Maurya (321 BCE) with help of Kautilya/Chanakya after defeating Dhana Nanda
Arthashastra by Kautilya: 15 books; covers statecraft, economy, military, spying — 'world's first treatise on political economy'
Chandragupta: Defeated Seleucus Nicator 305 BCE; got Hindu Kush region; married his daughter; converted to Jainism; died at Shravanabelagola
Bindusara (298–272 BCE): Called 'Amitraghata'; maintained Greek contacts; son Ashoka was governor of Taxila
Ashoka the Great (268–232 BCE): Kalinga War 261 BCE; 100,000 killed, 150,000 deported; converted to Buddhism
Ashoka's Dhamma: Non-violence, tolerance, vegetarianism, respect for elders, truthfulness
Rock Edicts: 14 major + 2 minor; Pillar Edicts: 7 major; Cave Inscriptions: 3 (for Ajivika monks)
Megasthenes (Greek ambassador): Wrote Indica; described 7 castes in Pataliputra; first foreign account of India
cards.alert: ⭐ Ashoka's inscriptions written in Brahmi (most), Kharosthi (NW), Greek & Aramaic (Afghanistan). James Prinsep deciphered Brahmi in 1837. National Emblem taken from Ashoka's Sarnath Pillar (4 lions).

cards.heading: Post-Mauryan Period (185 BCE – 300 CE)
points: Shunga dynasty (185–73 BCE): Pushyamitra Shunga killed last Mauryan; performed Ashvamedha; anti-Buddhist (Sanchi stupa damaged then restored)
Kanva dynasty (73–28 BCE): 4 kings; replaced by Satavahanas
Satavahana dynasty: Longest ruling dynasty of Deccan (230 BCE–220 CE); Simuka = founder; important kings: Gautamiputra Satakarni
Indo-Greek (Bactrian Greeks): Menander (Milinda) converted to Buddhism; Milindapanha — dialogue with Nagasena; introduced Hellenistic art
Shakas (Scythians): Important in NW India; era started 78 CE (Shaka Samvat = Indian national calendar)
Kushanas: Kujula Kadphises founded; Kanishka I — greatest Kushana; 4th Buddhist Council at Kashmir; Gandhara art flourished
Kanishka's era: 78 CE (some historians); patron of Mahayana Buddhism; coins show Buddha, Greek gods
Sangam Age (300 BCE–300 CE): Three Tamil kingdoms — Cheras (Vanji), Cholas (Uraiyur/Kaveripattinam), Pandyas (Madurai)

cards.heading: Gupta Empire — Golden Age (320–550 CE)
points: Sri Gupta (240–280 CE): founder; Ghatotkachagupta son; Chandragupta I took Maharajadhiraja title
Chandragupta I (319–335 CE): Married Kumaradevi (Lichchhavi princess); Gupta Era begins 319/320 CE
Samudragupta (335–375 CE): 'Napoleon of India' — V.A. Smith; Allahabad Pillar inscription (Harishena composed); Ashvamedha performed; great poet/musician
Chandragupta II / Vikramaditya (375–415 CE): Defeated Shakas of W. India; Silver coins; Fa Hien visited; Navratnas at court
Kumaragupta I (415–455 CE): Founded Nalanda University
Skandagupta (455–467 CE): Defeated Huns (Hunas); last great Gupta king
Decline: White Hun (Huna) invasions, internal rebellions, rise of feudal chiefs

rows: Zero, decimal, π=3.1416, Earth rotates, solar eclipse cause, Aryabhatiya

cards.alert: ⭐ Fa Hien (399–414 CE): Chinese Buddhist pilgrim; described Gupta India as peaceful, prosperous; people kind and vegetarian; no capital punishment. Gupta coins: gold = Dinar; silver = Rupyaka.

cards.heading: Harshavardhana (606–647 CE) & Post-Gupta
points: Harsha of Pushyabhuti/Vardhana dynasty; capital Kanauj; ruled vast empire
Xuanzang (Hiuen Tsang): Chinese pilgrim; visited Nalanda; wrote 'Si-Yu-Ki' (Record of Western Regions); 'Prince of Pilgrims'
Harsha: himself a playwright — Ratnavali, Priyadarshika, Nagananda; patron of scholars
Harshacharita: Biography by Banabhatta
Defeat: By Pulakeshi II (Chalukya king) at Battle of Narmada (618 CE) — Harsha could not cross Deccan
After Harsha's death: India broke into regional kingdoms
Pallavas (Kanchipuram): Mahendravarman I, Narasimhavarman I (Mamalla); Dravidian temple architecture began
Chalukyas (Badami): Pulakeshi II (greatest); Aihole inscription; Vesara style temples
Rashtrakutas: Dantidurga founded; Amoghavarsha (greatest); Ellora caves; defeated Gurjara-Pratiharas

cards.heading: Arab Conquest of Sindh & Early Invasions
points: Muhammad bin Qasim (712 CE): Defeated Dahir (ruler of Sindh); first permanent Muslim conquest in India
Arab conquest limited to Sindh; could not penetrate further due to resistance from Pratiharas and Rashtrakutas
Mahmud of Ghazni (997–1030): 17 invasions into India (1000–1027 CE); looted wealth; destroyed Somnath temple (1025 CE)
Alberuni (Al-Biruni): Came with Mahmud; wrote Kitab-ul-Hind (Tahqiq-i-Hind) — encyclopedic study of India
Muhammad Ghori (1173–1206): Aimed at permanent conquest, not just plunder

rows: Ghori defeated Prithviraj; turning point — Delhi Sultanate established
Ghori defeated Jaichandra (Gahadavala king of Kannauj)
Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi — Mughal Empire begins

cards.heading: Delhi Sultanate — Slave/Mamluk Dynasty (1206–1290)
points: Qutb-ud-din Aibak (1206–1210): First Sultan of Delhi; slave of Muhammad Ghori; 'Lakh Baksh' (giver of lakhs); started Qutb Minar; died playing chaugan (polo)
Iltutmish (1211–1236): Real founder of Delhi Sultanate; completed Qutb Minar; introduced iqta system; Shamsi group of 40 nobles (Chalisa)
Razia Sultana (1236–1240): First female ruler of Delhi; deposed by Chalisa nobles
Ghiyas-ud-din Balban (1266–1286): Destroyed Chalisa; theory of divine right of kings (Zil-e-Ilahi); Persian court etiquette (sijda, paibos); iron-and-blood policy
Qutb Minar: Started by Aibak, completed by Iltutmish; 4 storeys (5th added by Firoz Shah Tughlaq); 72.5m tall; inscribed UNESCO
cards.alert: ⭐ Iltutmish introduced: Silver Tanka (currency), Iqta system (revenue assignment), standardized coinage. Balban's court was modeled on Sassanid Persia — 'Nauroz' festival introduced.

cards.heading: Delhi Sultanate — Khilji Dynasty (1290–1320)
points: Jalal-ud-din Khilji (1290–1296): First Khilji Sultan; mild and generous; killed by nephew Alauddin
Alauddin Khilji (1296–1316): Greatest Khilji sultan; conquered Deccan (Malik Kafur as general); Market Control Reforms
Market Reforms: 4 markets — grain, cattle, cloth, horses/cattle/slaves; Shahna (controller) + Barids (spies) + Munhiyans (secret agents)
Price control success: Fixed prices, ensured supply; soldiers could maintain large army at low cost
Military reforms: First to introduce da'gh (branding of horses) and chehra (muster roll) system
Mongol invasions: Repelled 6 major Mongol invasions; built defensive walls
Malik Kafur: Eunuch general; conquered Devagiri (1307), Warangal (1309), Dwarsamudra, Madurai (1311)
cards.alert: ⭐ Alauddin's Market Control — described by Ziauddin Barani in 'Tarikh-i-Firoz Shahi'. 'Sijda' (prostration before sultan) and 'Paibos' (kissing feet) — court etiquette. Khilji revolution: first non-Turkish Muslim dynasty.

cards.heading: Delhi Sultanate — Tughlaq Dynasty (1320–1414)
points: Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq (1320–1325): Founded dynasty; built Tughlaqabad; died in accident (Muhammed Tughlaq suspected)
Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1325–1351): Genius-but-erratic; 5 controversial experiments:
1. Transfer of Capital: Delhi → Daulatabad (Devagiri) — massive failure; people died on 1500 km march
2. Token Currency: Bronze coins instead of gold/silver — forged massively; withdrawn
3. Taxation in Doab: Increased land tax; famine followed; rebellion
4. Khurasan Expedition: Planned, never executed
5. Qarachil Expedition: Against Chinese-controlled territory; disaster
Ibn Battuta: Moroccan traveler; visited during Muhammad bin Tughlaq; wrote Rihla; appointed Qazi of Delhi

Firoz Shah Tughlaq (1351–1388): Welfare measures; 1200 fruit gardens; canals (first major canal system); abolished torture; hospitals (shifa khanas); 24 new towns including Firozabad, Jaunpur, Fatehabad
Timur's invasion (1398): Sacked Delhi; Tughlaq empire collapsed

cards.heading: Vijayanagara Empire (1336–1646)
points: Founded by Harihara I and Bukka Raya (brothers) at Hampi on Tungabhadra; inspired by Vidyaranya (saint)
Four dynasties: Sangama → Saluva → Tuluva → Aravidu
Krishnadevaraya (1509–1529): Greatest ruler; Tuluva dynasty; conquered Raichur Doab; patronized Telugu literature; himself wrote Amuktamalyada (Telugu) and Jambavathi Kalyanam (Sanskrit)
Ashta Diggajas: 8 great Telugu poets at Krishnadevaraya's court; Allasani Peddana = 'Andhra Kavita Pitamaha'
Battle of Talikota (1565): Vijayanagara defeated by Deccan Sultanates coalition (Bijapur, Bidar, Ahmadnagar, Golconda); Rama Raya killed; Hampi sacked
Architecture: Dravidian style; Vittala Temple (stone chariot); Hazara Rama Temple; gopurams (gateway towers)
cards.alert: ⭐ Domingo Paes and Fernão Nunes: Portuguese travelers who visited Vijayanagara and gave detailed accounts. Hampi is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1986). Tungabhadra river = lifeline of Vijayanagara.

cards.heading: Bhakti Movement
points: Background: Reaction to caste rigidity, Brahmanical domination, and desire for direct personal devotion to God
South India pioneers (Alvars & Nayanmars): 6th–9th CE; Tamil devotional poetry
Ramanuja (1017–1137 CE): Vishishtadvaita (qualified non-dualism); devotion to Vishnu; opposed caste discrimination
Madhvacharya (1238–1317): Dvaita (dualism); devotion to Vishnu
Adi Shankaracharya (788–820 CE): Advaita Vedanta (non-dualism); Brahman is the only reality; established 4 mathas (Sringeri, Dwaraka, Puri, Badrinath)
Ramananda (14th–15th CE): Disciple of Ramanuja; preached in Hindi; accepted all castes; guru of Kabir, Raidas, Dhanna
Kabirdas (1440–1518): Weaver; nirguni bhakti; unity of Hinduism-Islam; Bijak; dohas; criticized rituals and caste
Guru Nanak (1469–1539): Founded Sikhism; Ik Onkar (one God); Japji Sahib; Guru Granth Sahib (compiled by Guru Arjan Dev)
Mirabai (1498–1546): Rajput princess; Krishna devotee; Meera ke Pad
Tukaram (1608–1649): Maharashtra; Abhangas; Warkari sect; devotion to Vitthal

cards.heading: Mughal Empire — Babur to Akbar
points: Babur (1526–1530): Descendant of Timur (father) and Genghis Khan (mother); from Fergana (Uzbekistan); defeated Ibrahim Lodi at Panipat I (1526); used gunpowder/artillery for first time in India; wrote Baburnama (autobiography in Chagatai Turkish)

Humayun (1530–1556 with interruption): Lost to Sher Shah Suri at Chausa (1539) and Kanauj (1540); exile in Persia 15 years; regained throne 1555; died falling from library steps; 'stumbled out of life as he stumbled through it' — Lane-Poole
Sher Shah Suri (1540–1545): Greatest Afghan ruler; Grand Trunk Road (Peshawar to Sonargaon); Rupee currency; Rohtas Fort; land revenue reforms (measurement + fixed rate); postal system (dak chowkis)

Akbar (1556–1605): Battle of Panipat II (1556) vs Hemu — Bairam Khan as regent; built Fatehpur Sikri; Navratnas; Din-i-Ilahi (1582); abolished Jizyah; Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) for religious debate; Sulh-i-Kul (peace with all)

cards.heading: Mughal Empire — Jahangir to Aurangzeb
points: Jahangir (1605–1627): 'Nur-ud-din Muhammad Jahangir'; married Nur Jahan (real power behind throne); patronized painting (Mansur — animal painter; Bichitr); Thomas Roe's embassy (1615) seeking trade rights

Shah Jahan (1628–1658): Golden age of Mughal architecture; Taj Mahal (built 1632–1653 in memory of Mumtaz Mahal); Red Fort (Delhi); Jama Masjid; Peacock Throne; Shah Jahan Masjid; Moti Masjid; war of succession — imprisoned by son Aurangzeb

Aurangzeb (1658–1707): Most controversial Mughal; reimposed Jizyah (1679); banned music and dance at court; Alamgir (world conqueror); annexed Bijapur (1686) and Golconda (1687); 25 years in Deccan against Marathas — 'the war that wrecked the Mughal empire'
Shivaji Maharaj: Founded Maratha kingdom; guerrilla warfare (ganimi kava); captured numerous forts; crowned at Raigad (1674) as Chhatrapati; Ashtapradhan (8-minister council)
Decline of Mughals: Aurangzeb's long Deccan wars, Nadir Shah's invasion (1739), Ahmed Shah Abdali's invasions, rise of Marathas, Sikhs, Jats
cards.alert: ⭐ Taj Mahal architects: Ustad Ahmad Lahauri (chief); 20,000 workers; 22 years to build; white marble from Makrana (Rajasthan). Aurangzeb's tomb at Khuldabad (Maharashtra) — simple and unadorned (per his wishes).

cards.heading: Sufi Movement
points: Origin: Islamic mysticism; 'Sufi' from 'suf' (wool — coarse garment worn by mystics) or 'safa' (purity)
Core belief: Personal union with God through love and devotion; rejected formal rituals
Silsilas (Orders): Hereditary chains of masters (Pirs/Shaykhs) and disciples (Murids); khanqahs (hospices) were centers
Chishti Order: Most popular in India; Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti (Ajmer); Qutb-ud-din Bakhtiyar Kaki (Delhi); Farid-ud-din Ganj-i-Shakar (Pak Pattan — Punjab); Nizamuddin Auliya (Delhi)
Suhrawardi Order: Less popular; Abu Hafs Umar Suhrawardi; active in Punjab and Sindh
Qadiri Order: Abdul Qadir Gilani; Shah Badakhshani in India; Dara Shikoh a follower
Naqshbandi Order: Most orthodox; Sheikh Ahmad Sirhindi (opposed Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi); Baqi Billah in India
Influence: Broke caste barriers; used vernacular languages; qawwali (devotional music); contributed to Urdu literature

cards.heading: Maratha Empire & Peshwas
points: Shivaji (1627–1680): Born at Shivneri Fort; mother Jijabai; mentor Dadoji Kondadev; crowned 1674 at Raigad; Swarajya (self-rule) concept

Shivaji's administration: Ashtapradhan — Peshwa (PM), Amatya (finance), Sachiv (correspondence), Mantri (chronicles), Senapati (army chief), Sumant (foreign minister), Nyayadhish (justice), Panditrao (religious)
Battle of Pratapgad (1659): Shivaji killed Afzal Khan; turning point
Sambhaji (1680–1689): Shivaji's son; killed by Aurangzeb
Peshwa era: Balaji Vishwanath (1713 — first Peshwa); Baji Rao I (1720–1740 — greatest general; never lost a battle); Balaji Baji Rao (Nana Sahib) — peak Maratha expansion
Battle of Panipat III (1761): Maratha Confederacy defeated by Ahmed Shah Abdali (Afghan); Vishwasrao (Peshwa's son) and Bhau Saheb (commander) killed; 'Panipat that broke Maratha power'
British and Maratha Wars: 3 Anglo-Maratha Wars (1775–1818); 3rd war ended Peshwa rule
cards.alert: ⭐ Shivaji's navy: First organized naval force; Kanhoji Angre — greatest Maratha admiral. Shivaji used hill forts strategically — controlled 300+ forts. Revenue system: Chauth (1/4 of land revenue) and Sardeshmukhi (1/10 extra).

cards.heading: European Trading Companies in India
points: Vasco da Gama: Reached Calicut (Kozhikode) May 1498; opened sea route via Cape of Good Hope; welcomed by Zamorin
Portuguese: Established first European trading post at Calicut; Goa captured by Albuquerque (1510); Diu (1535); Estado da India = Portuguese India; brought Christianity, introduced printing press
Dutch (VOC): Est. 1602; captured some Portuguese stations; main interest in SE Asia; defeated at Battle of Bedara (1759) by English

British East India Company (EIC): Chartered by Elizabeth I on 31 December 1600; first factory at Surat (1608); Fort St. George (Madras, 1639); Fort William (Calcutta, 1690); Bombay (gifted by Portuguese to British Crown 1661)
French (Compagnie des Indes): Est. 1664; Pondicherry (1673) headquarters; Dupleix — greatest French governor; lost Carnatic Wars to British
Danish: Tranquebar and Serampore (Srirampur); not significant commercially; missionaries (Bible translation)

cards.heading: Carnatic Wars & Expansion of British Power
points: 1st Carnatic War (1746–1748): French (Dupleix) vs British; French captured Madras; ended by Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle; Madras returned to British
2nd Carnatic War (1749–1754): Robert Clive's defense of Arcot (1751) — turning point; British supported Muhammed Ali vs Chanda Sahib (French-backed)

3rd Carnatic War (1756–1763): Part of Seven Years War; Lally (French) vs Eyre Coote (British); Battle of Wandiwash (1760) — decisive British victory; Treaty of Paris 1763 — French restricted to 5 cities without military power
Battle of Plassey (1757): Clive defeated Siraj-ud-Daulah (Nawab of Bengal); helped by Mir Jafar (betrayal); established British dominance in Bengal
Black Hole of Calcutta (1756): Siraj-ud-Daulah imprisoned 146 British in a small cell; 123 died (disputed account)
Battle of Buxar (1764): British defeated joint forces of Mir Qasim, Nawab of Awadh, and Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II; more significant than Plassey — proved British military superiority
Dual Government in Bengal (1765–1772): EIC had diwani (revenue) rights; Nawab retained nizamat (administration); result: massive exploitation, Bengal Famine of 1770 (10 million died)

cards.heading: Major British Policies & Reforms
points: Regulating Act 1773: First interference of British Parliament in EIC; Governor of Bengal became Governor-General; Warren Hastings = first GG
Pitt's India Act 1784: Dual control — Board of Control (political) + Court of Directors (commercial); political matters under Crown

Subsidiary Alliance (1798–1805, Lord Wellesley): Native ruler kept British troops and paid for them; ceded territory if unable to pay; surrendered control of foreign relations; princes became subordinate; adopted by Hyderabad, Mysore, Awadh, Maratha chiefs

Doctrine of Lapse (1848–1856, Lord Dalhousie): State without natural heir 'lapsed' to British; annexed: Satara (1848), Jaitpur, Sambalpur (1849), Baghat (1850), Udaipur (1852), Jhansi (1853), Nagpur (1854), Awadh (1856 — 'misgovernance')
Charter Act 1813: Ended EIC trade monopoly (except tea and China trade); missionaries allowed; ₹1 lakh for Indian education
Charter Act 1833: EIC became purely administrative body; Governor-General of Bengal became Governor-General of India (Lord William Bentinck = first)
Charter Act 1853: Last Charter Act; introduced competitive exam for ICS; legislative + executive functions separated

cards.heading: Social Reforms & British Education Policy
points: Sati Abolition Act 1829: Lord William Bentinck; campaigned by Raja Ram Mohan Roy; Brahmo Samaj (1828) founded by Roy
Widow Remarriage Act 1856: Lord Dalhousie; campaigned by Pandit Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar
Female Infanticide Abolition Act 1870
Child Marriage Restraint Act (Sarda Act) 1929: Min age — girls 14, boys 18
Macaulay's Minute (1835): English medium education; 'filtration theory' — educate upper class who would educate lower; created 'Indian in blood and colour, British in taste and opinion'
Wood's Education Despatch 1854: 'Magna Carta of Indian education'; universities at Bombay, Calcutta, Madras (1857); vernacular education emphasized
Orientalists vs Anglicists debate: Orientalists (Sanskrit/Arabic learning) vs Anglicists (English education); Anglicists won with Macaulay
cards.alert: ⭐ Raja Ram Mohan Roy = 'Father of Modern India'; first Indian to use term 'Hinduism'; died in Bristol (England) 1833. Brahmo Samaj later split: Debendranath Tagore (Adi Brahmo Samaj) vs Keshab Chandra Sen (Brahmo Samaj of India).

cards.heading: Economic Impact of British Rule
points: Drain of Wealth: Dadabhai Naoroji coined the term in 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' (1901); estimated drain at £200 million annually; later calculated by R.C. Dutt
De-industrialization: Indian handicrafts destroyed by cheap machine-made British goods + discriminatory tariffs; weavers, artisans ruined; 'reverse drain'
Land Revenue Systems: Permanent Settlement (Bengal 1793 — Cornwallis); Ryotwari (Madras and Bombay — Thomas Munro); Mahalwari (NW India, Punjab — Holt Mackenzie)
Famines under British: Great Bengal Famine 1770 (10 million); Orissa Famine 1866; Rajputana Famine 1869; Bihar Famine 1874; Great Famine 1876–78; Deccan Famine 1896–97; Bengal Famine 1943 (3 million)
Railways: First railway 1853 (Bombay–Thane, 34 km); by 1905 = 44,000 km; helped British drain resources, not Indian development
International trade: India became supplier of raw materials (cotton, jute, indigo, opium) and buyer of British manufactured goods

cards.heading: Revolt of 1857 — First War of Independence
points: Immediate cause: Enfield P-53 rifle with cartridges greased with cow and pig fat; soldiers had to bite the cartridge
Started: 29 March 1857 — Mangal Pandey at Barrackpore; 10 May 1857 — Sepoy Mutiny at Meerut
Spread: Delhi, Lucknow, Kanpur, Jhansi, Bareilly, Allahabad, Faizabad

Leaders: Bahadur Shah Zafar II (Delhi — nominal leader), Rani Lakshmibai (Jhansi — 'India's Joan of Arc'), Nana Sahib (Kanpur), Tantia Tope (Kanpur/Central India), Begum Hazrat Mahal (Lucknow), Bakht Khan (Delhi military commander), Kunwar Singh (Bihar)

rows: Doctrine of Lapse, annexation of Awadh (1856), insult to Mughal dynasty
Drain of wealth, destruction of artisans, heavy taxation
Interference in religion (widow remarriage act, missionary activity), Sati abolition
Unequal pay, service in distant lands, racial discrimination, cartridge issue
Rumor that bones of cows/pigs mixed in flour supplied to soldiers

cards.alert: ⭐ Result of 1857: EIC rule ended; Crown rule began — Government of India Act 1858; Secretary of State for India created with 15-member Council; Viceroy replaced Governor-General; Queen Victoria's Proclamation promised equal treatment. Disraeli called it 'Sepoy Mutiny'; Savarkar — '1st War of Independence'; SN Sen — 'Mutiny'; RC Majumdar — 'neither first, nor national, nor war of independence'.

cards.heading: Early Nationalism — Moderates (1885–1905)
points: Indian National Congress founded: 28 December 1885; Bombay (Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College); AO Hume (retired ICS), Dadabhai Naoroji, Dinshaw Wacha instrumental
First president: W.C. Bonnerjee; first session: Bombay (72 delegates)
Moderates' methods: Constitutional agitation, petitions, prayers, delegations to Britain; believed in British justice

Moderate leaders: Dadabhai Naoroji ('Grand Old Man of India'; MP in British Parliament 1892), Gopal Krishna Gokhale ('political guru of Gandhi'; Servants of India Society 1905), Pherozeshah Mehta, Surendranath Banerjee
Moderate demands: Expansion of legislative councils, separation of judiciary from executive, reduction of military expenditure, India Council reform
Moderate achievement: Created national consciousness, trained leaders, exposed British economic exploitation through data
INC in early years: 'a three-day tamasha' — Bal Gangadhar Tilak's criticism of Moderates' limited approach

cards.heading: Extremists & Swadeshi Movement (1905–1918)
points: Partition of Bengal (1905): Lord Curzon divided Bengal on 16 October 1905 (into Hindu-majority West Bengal + Muslim-majority East Bengal); 'divide and rule'; united Hindus and Muslims initially against it
Swadeshi Movement: Boycott of British goods; use of Indian goods; burning of foreign cloth
Bal-Pal-Lal: Bal Gangadhar Tilak (Pune), Bipin Chandra Pal (Bengal), Lala Lajpat Rai (Punjab) — extremist trio
Tilak's slogans: 'Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it'; started Ganesh Chaturthi and Shivaji festivals as national events; Kesari and Mahratta newspapers
Annulment of Partition: 1911 — Bengal reunited; capital shifted from Calcutta to Delhi
Muslim League founded: 1906 at Dhaka; Aga Khan; separate electorate demand
Morley-Minto Reforms (1909): Introduced communal electorate for Muslims — 'poisonous in principle' according to Gokhale
Lucknow Pact (1916): Congress-League unity; joint demands for self-government; Congress accepted separate electorates temporarily
cards.alert: ⭐ Vande Mataram: Written by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay in 'Anandamath' (1882); set to music by Tagore; became anthem of Swadeshi Movement. Only first two stanzas adopted as national song (1950).

cards.heading: Gandhian Era — First Phase (1917–1922)
points: Gopal Krishna Gokhale invited Gandhi to return from South Africa; Gandhi agreed to observe India for one year before taking political positions
Champaran Satyagraha (1917): Against Tinkathia system (indigo planters forced farmers to cultivate 3/20 of land with indigo); Gandhi's first satyagraha in India; won partial concession
Kheda Satyagraha (1918): Revenue suspension for drought-affected peasants of Kheda (Gujarat); Gandhi won
Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918): Gandhi's first hunger strike; on behalf of textile workers; settled with 35% wage increase
Rowlatt Act (1919): Black Act; allowed detention without trial for 2 years; Gandhi called for hartal (strike)

Jallianwala Bagh Massacre (13 April 1919): General Dyer ordered firing on unarmed crowd (Baisakhi gathering); 379 killed (official), ~1000+ according to Congress; Hunter Committee inquiry; Udham Singh avenged in 1940 (shot O'Dwyer in London)
Khilafat Movement (1920): Gandhi allied with Muslim leaders Ali brothers (Muhammad Ali & Shaukat Ali) to protest dismemberment of Ottoman Caliphate; Hindu-Muslim unity

Non-Cooperation Movement (1920–1922): Boycott of British goods, courts, educational institutions, government jobs; return of titles; Gandhi stopped it after Chauri Chaura (4 Feb 1922 — mob burned police station, 22 police killed)

cards.heading: Civil Disobedience & Round Table Conferences (1928–1934)
points: Simon Commission (1927): All-British; 'Simon Go Back' protests; Lala Lajpat Rai lathi-charged in Lahore; died November 1928; Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, Sukhdev — arrested for killing Saunders
Nehru Report (1928): Motilal Nehru's report proposing Dominion Status (not Poorna Swaraj); Jinnah rejected communal proposals
Poorna Swaraj Resolution: 31 December 1929, Lahore session, Jawaharlal Nehru as Congress president; 26 January 1930 declared as Poorna Swaraj Day (Independence Day)
Dandi March / Salt Satyagraha: 12 March – 6 April 1930; Gandhi walked 241 miles from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi (Gujarat coast); broke salt law on 6 April; 'most creative act of disobedience'
Civil Disobedience Movement: Spread across India; forest laws broken, liquor shops picketed
Round Table Conferences: 1st (1930) — Congress absent; 2nd (1931) — Gandhi-Irwin Pact led to Gandhi attending; 3rd (1932) — Congress absent (Gandhi arrested)
Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931): 'March to talks' — civil disobedience suspended; political prisoners released; Congress allowed to make salt for personal use; Gandhi agreed to attend 2nd RTC
Communal Award (1932): Ramsay MacDonald — separate electorates for Dalits; Gandhi's fast-unto-death; Poona Pact (September 1932) — reserved seats for Dalits within Hindu electorate (not separate)

cards.heading: Quit India Movement & Final Phase (1939–1947)
points: Congress and WW2: Congress demanded independence declaration before cooperation; Viceroy Linlithgow declared India at war without consulting Congress; Congress ministries resigned (1939)
Pakistan Resolution (1940): Lahore; Muslim League demanded separate state for Muslims
August Offer (1940): Linlithgow offered expanded Viceroy's council, promise of Dominion Status after war — rejected by Congress
Cripps Mission (1942): Stafford Cripps; offered Dominion Status after war + right of provinces to secede; Gandhi called it 'post-dated cheque on a failing bank'; rejected

Quit India Movement (8 August 1942): Bombay session; 'Do or Die'; Gandhi's speech at Gowalia Tank Maidan; Gandhi and all leaders arrested the very next day; underground leadership — JP Narayan, Ram Manohar Lohia, Aruna Asaf Ali; parallel governments at Ballia, Satara, Midnapore

INA (Indian National Army): Rash Behari Bose organized; Subhas Chandra Bose took over 1943; 'Jai Hind'; 'Delhi Chalo'; INA Trials (1945) — Red Fort; made Nehru don barrister's gown again; public sympathy with INA
Cabinet Mission Plan (1946): 3-tier federation (Centre–Groupings–Provinces); Congress accepted with reservations; Jinnah and League accepted, then rejected; interim government formed
Independence & Partition: Mountbatten Plan (3 June 1947); Indian Independence Act (18 July 1947); 15 August 1947 — Independence; partition into India + Pakistan (Radcliffe Line)

cards.heading: Revolutionary Nationalism
points: Bal Gangadhar Tilak (1856–1920): Founded Home Rule League (1916); defended himself in Sedition trial 1897 and 1908; wrote 'Gita Rahasya' in prison (Mandalay); died 1 August 1920 — Gandhi called his death 'a debt we cannot repay'
Lala Lajpat Rai (1865–1928): 'Punjab Kesari'; founded Punjab National Bank; wrote 'Unhappy India'; died after police lathi charge
Bipin Chandra Pal (1858–1932): 'Father of revolutionary thoughts'; advocated passive resistance
Aurobindo Ghosh (1872–1950): Alipore bomb case (1908); spiritual retreat to Pondicherry; founded Auroville
HSRA (Hindustan Socialist Republican Association): Founded 1928 by Bhagat Singh, Chandrashekhar Azad, Sukhdev, Rajguru; opposed to non-violence
Bhagat Singh (1907–1931): Lahore Conspiracy Case; threw bombs in Central Assembly (8 April 1929) — 'to make the deaf hear'; hanged 23 March 1931; youngest martyr at 23
Chandrashekhar Azad (1906–1931): 'Azad' (never arrested alive); shot himself at Alfred Park, Allahabad on 27 February 1931

Subhas Chandra Bose (1897–1945?): 'Netaji'; elected Congress president 1938; resigned over differences; founded Forward Bloc; escaped house arrest; reached Germany then Japan; reorganized INA; 'Give me blood, I will give you freedom'; disappeared after plane crash near Taipei (1945)
cards.alert: ⭐ Kakori Conspiracy (1925): Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqullah Khan, Roshan Singh, Rajendra Lahiri — looted British treasury train near Kakori; hanged. Chittagong Armoury Raid (1930): Surya Sen (Master Da) led attack on British armoury in Chittagong.

cards.heading: Peasant, Tribal & Workers' Movements
points: Indigo Revolt (1859–60): Bengal; against forced indigo cultivation; first major peasant revolt; Nil Darpan (play by Dinabandhu Mitra) exposed conditions
Deccan Riots (1875): Poona and Ahmadnagar; peasants against moneylenders; looted records (loan bonds)
Pabna Agrarian League (1873): Bengal; against zamindars' illegal levies
Mappila Rebellion (Moplah Revolt, 1921): Kerala; Muslim peasants against Hindu landlords; turned violent; suppressed brutally
Eka Movement (1921–22): UP; peasants under Madari Pasi; against high rents
Bardoli Satyagraha (1928): Gujarat; under Vallabhbhai Patel; against 30% land revenue increase; revenue suspended; Bardoli women gave Patel title 'Sardar'
Tebhaga Movement (1946): Bengal; sharecroppers demanded 2/3 share (from 1/2) of crop
Workers' Movements: All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) — 1920; Bombay Mill Strike 1919; Jamshedpur Strike 1920; influence of Marxism after Russian Revolution 1917
Tribal Revolts: Santhal Hul (1855), Munda Rebellion (Birsa Munda 1899–1900 — 'Ulgulan'), Rampa Rebellion (1879–80), Kol Rebellion (1831–32)

cards.heading: Women in Freedom Struggle
points: Annie Besant (1847–1933): Irish-British; Theosophical Society; founded Home Rule League (1916); first woman president of INC (1917)
Sarojini Naidu (1879–1949): 'Nightingale of India'; poet; participated in Non-Cooperation, Civil Disobedience, Quit India; first Indian woman president of INC (1925); first woman Governor (UP, 1947)
Kasturba Gandhi (1869–1944): Participated in all of Gandhi's campaigns; died in Aga Khan Palace (Pune) during Quit India imprisonment
Aruna Asaf Ali (1909–1996): 'Heroine of Quit India'; hoisted Congress flag at Gowalia Tank 1942 when leaders arrested; underground leader
Usha Mehta (1920–2000): Operated secret Congress Radio during Quit India (1942)
Rani Lakshmibai (1828–1858): Fought British in 1857; died at Gwalior; 'Khoob ladi mardani woh to Jhansi wali rani thi' — Subhadra Kumari Chauhan
Captain Lakshmi Sahgal: Commanded Rani Jhansi Regiment of INA

cards.heading: Integration of Princely States
points: On 15 August 1947: 562 princely states; given choice to join India, Pakistan, or remain independent
Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (Iron Man of India) + V.P. Menon (Secretary, States Ministry) — masterminded integration
Most states joined voluntarily; Standstill Agreement signed first, then Instrument of Accession

Problematic cases: Junagarh (Muslim ruler, Hindu majority — people's vote for India), Hyderabad (Muslim Nizam, Hindu majority — Police action 'Operation Polo' September 1948), Kashmir (Hindu Maharaja Hari Singh, Muslim majority — acceded on 26 Oct 1947 after Pakistani tribal raid; Instrument of Accession signed)
Kashmir accession conditions: Accession of defence, foreign affairs, communications; rest with state; basis of Art. 370 (now abrogated 5 August 2019)
Goa, Daman, Diu: Portuguese colonies; liberated by military action ('Operation Vijay') December 1961; 37th amendment — added to India
Pondicherry: French colony; transferred to India November 1954
cards.alert: ⭐ Sardar Patel called 'Bismarck of India' for unifying princely states, just as Bismarck unified Germany. Patel also directed the drafting of Part V (Union) and Part VI (States) of the Constitution.

cards.heading: Constitutional Development & First Government
points: Constituent Assembly: Elected by Provincial Assemblies; 299 members; Cabinet Mission Plan; first met 9 December 1946; Dr. Rajendra Prasad = President
Drafting Committee: Chairman Dr. B.R. Ambedkar; 7 members including Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar, T.T. Krishnamachari, N. Gopalaswami Ayyangar
Constitution adopted 26 November 1949; came into force 26 January 1950
First General Election (1951–52): 489 seats; universal adult franchise for first time in India; INC won 364 seats; voter turnout 45.7%; Sukumar Sen = first Chief Election Commissioner
Jawaharlal Nehru: First PM; served 16 years, 9 months (longest); non-aligned movement (Panchsheel — 5 principles of peaceful coexistence); planned economic development; socialist pattern
Panchsheel (1954): Signed with China; 5 principles — mutual respect, non-aggression, non-interference, equality, peaceful coexistence; violated by China in 1962
Sino-Indian War (1962): China attacked; India unprepared; lost Aksai Chin (Ladakh); massive national humiliation; Nehru broken; died May 1964

cards.heading: Five Year Plans & Economic Development
points: Planning Commission: Est. 1950; PM as chairman; Five Year Plans
1st FYP (1951–56): Focus on agriculture; Bhakra Nangal, Hirakud dams; exceeded targets
2nd FYP (1956–61): Mahalanobis model; heavy industry (Bhilai, Durgapur, Rourkela steel plants); Rapid Industrialization strategy
3rd FYP (1961–66): War years (1962, 1965); drought; failed badly; 3 Annual Plans (Plan Holiday 1966–69)
4th FYP (1969–74): Green Revolution consolidation; Garibi Hatao (Indira Gandhi slogan)

Green Revolution (late 1960s): M.S. Swaminathan; HYV seeds (IR-8 rice, Lerma Rojo wheat); Punjab, Haryana, western UP; India achieved food self-sufficiency by 1970s; negative: regional disparity, pesticide overuse, groundwater depletion
LPG Reforms (1991): Liberalisation, Privatisation, Globalisation; PM Narasimha Rao + Finance Minister Manmohan Singh; India opened economy during BOP crisis
NITI Aayog (2015): Replaced Planning Commission; not a constitutional body; PM as chairman; think tank + policy making

cards.heading: Major Wars & Foreign Policy
points: Indo-Pak War 1947–48: Over Kashmir; ceasefire 1 January 1949; UN Resolution for plebiscite (never held)
Sino-Indian War 1962: 20 October – 21 November 1962; India lost Aksai Chin; Henderson Brooks Report (classified)
Indo-Pak War 1965: Over Rann of Kutch and Kashmir; ended with Tashkent Declaration (10 January 1966) — PM Shastri died in Tashkent
Indo-Pak War 1971: Bangladesh Liberation War; India helped Mukti Bahini; 16 December 1971 — Pakistani Army surrendered (90,000 POWs); Shimla Agreement (1972) — bilateral resolution of Kashmir
Pokhran Nuclear Test I (1974): Smiling Buddha; PV Narasimha Rao government test; India became 6th nuclear power
Pokhran II (1998): Operation Shakti; Vajpayee government; 5 tests; India declared nuclear state; US sanctions
Kargil War (1999): Pakistani soldiers occupied peaks in Kargil; 'Operation Vijay' — India recaptured; Pervez Musharraf responsible; India won diplomatically and militarily

cards.heading: Political History — Key Events
points: States Reorganisation Act 1956: Linguistic reorganisation; Fazl Ali Commission (SRC 1953); created 14 states + 6 UTs

Emergency (1975–1977): Article 352; Indira Gandhi declared on 25 June 1975 citing internal disturbance; Allahabad HC declared her election void; press censorship, mass arrests (JP Narayan, Vajpayee, Advani); 21-point programme; ended March 1977; Janata Party won

Operation Blue Star (1984): Army stormed Golden Temple (Amritsar) to flush out Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale and militants (June 1984); Indira Gandhi assassinated by Sikh bodyguards (31 October 1984); anti-Sikh riots
Rajiv Gandhi era (1984–89): 73rd and 74th Amendment (Panchayati Raj, Municipalities); Bofors scandal; Punjab accord (1985); Assam accord (1985); Sri Lanka (IPKF 1987–89)
Mandal Commission (1992): OBC reservations implemented by VP Singh; 27% reservation for OBCs; Mandal agitation; counter-agitation — 'Kamandal' (BJP's Ayodhya movement)
Ayodhya (1992): Babri Masjid demolished 6 December 1992; riots; Supreme Court verdict 2019 — Ram Lalla gets the disputed land

cards.heading: Rock-Cut Architecture & Ancient Temples
points: Ajanta Caves (Maharashtra): 30 Buddhist caves; 2nd BCE – 7th CE; paintings depicting Jataka tales and Buddha's life; UNESCO WHS
Ellora Caves (Maharashtra): 34 caves — Buddhist (12) + Hindu (17) + Jain (5); 6th–11th CE; Kailasha Temple (cave 16) — largest monolithic rock-cut structure; UNESCO WHS
Elephanta Caves (near Mumbai): 7 caves; mainly Hindu; Trimurti of Shiva (5m high) — most famous sculpture; UNESCO WHS
Mahabalipuram (Tamil Nadu): 7th–8th CE; Pallava period; Shore Temple; Pancha Rathas (5 monolithic temples); Arjuna's Penance (world's largest rock relief); UNESCO WHS
Badami Cave Temples (Karnataka): Chalukya period; 6th CE; 4 caves (3 Hindu + 1 Jain)
Udayagiri Caves (MP): Gupta period; Varaha panel (boar incarnation of Vishnu); most famous Gupta sculpture

cards.heading: Temple Architecture Styles

rows: Curvilinear shikhara (tower), amalaka (disc) on top, square sanctum
Pyramidal vimana (tower), gopuram (gateway), large walled complex

cards.alert: ⭐ Brihadeeswarar Temple (Thanjavur, 1010 CE) — built by Rajaraja Chola I; 66m high vimana; shadow of the shikhara does not fall on ground at noon; made entirely from granite (not locally available); UNESCO. Khajuraho (MP) — UNESCO; Chandela dynasty; erotic sculptures on outer walls, devotional inside.

cards.heading: Indian Painting Traditions
points: Prehistoric: Bhimbetka rock paintings (30,000+ years old); animals, hunting scenes, dancing
Ajanta Paintings: Tempera technique; two types — Jataka stories + scenes from Buddha's life; artists unknown

Mughal Painting: Persian + Indian fusion; portrait painting emerged; Mir Sayyid Ali and Abd al-Samad (first Mughal painters under Humayun); Abdus Samad, Daswant, Basawan (under Akbar); Abu'l Hasan (under Jahangir — Squirrel painting)
Rajput Painting: Sub-schools — Mewar, Marwar, Bundi-Kota, Amber-Jaipur; Krishna-Radha themes; Ragamala paintings (musical modes depicted visually)
Pahari Painting: Himachal Pradesh hills; Basohli (bold colors), Kangra (delicate, romantic); 17th–19th CE
Madhubani (Mithila) Painting: Bihar; geometric patterns; done by women; natural colors; on walls and floors; now on paper and canvas; GI tag
Warli Painting: Maharashtra tribal; geometric shapes (circles, triangles); white on red/brown; scenes of daily life; rice paste on mud walls
Kalamkari: Andhra Pradesh; hand-painted or block-printed; Srikalahasti style (freehand) + Machilipatnam style (block print); mythological scenes

cards.heading: Indian Music — Classical Traditions
points: Natya Shastra: Bharata Muni; 2nd BCE – 2nd CE; encyclopedic treatise on performing arts; basis of Indian classical music, dance, drama
Hindustani (North Indian) Classical Music: Influenced by Persian/Mughal; khyal, thumri, dhrupad, dadra; associated with ragas and talas
Carnatic (South Indian) Classical Music: Purer Dravidian tradition; Trinity — Tyagaraja, Muthuswami Dikshitar, Syama Sastri (19th CE Thanjavur)
Tansen: Greatest Mughal court musician; Darbari Kanada raga, Miyan ki Malhar; disciple of Haridas Swami
Ustad Bismillah Khan: Shehnai; Bharat Ratna 2001
Pandit Ravi Shankar: Sitar; introduced Indian music to West; Bharat Ratna 1999
M.S. Subbulakshmi: First musician to get Bharat Ratna (1998); Carnatic vocalist
Ghazal: Urdu poetry set to music; Mirza Ghalib (poetry); Mehdi Hassan, Jagjit Singh, Ghulam Ali (singers)

rows: Ancient; Devadasi tradition; revived 20th C by Rukmini Devi Arundale
Temple dance; Mughal court influence; 16th-18th CE
Fast footwork, spins, expressive facial mime
Temple tradition; sculptures of Konark & Puri; suppressed under British
Elaborate makeup + costumes; 12+ hours epics from Ramayana/Mahabharata
17th CE village (Kuchipudi) Brahmin men played all roles
Fast tempo, dramatic; Tarangam (brass plate dance)
Ancient; Vaishnavite influence; Rabindranath Tagore introduced to mainland India
16th CE; revived by Vallathol Narayana Menon
Lasya (feminine grace); white costume with gold border
Vaishnavite themes; till 2000 only male monks performed

cards.heading: Literature — Ancient to Medieval
points: Sanskrit Literature: Vedas (1500–500 BCE); Epics — Ramayana (Valmiki, 24,000 verses) and Mahabharata (Vyasa, 100,000 verses, world's longest epic)
Sangam Literature (300 BCE – 300 CE): Tamil; 2381 poems; 8 anthologies (Ettuthokai) + 10 idylls; themes: love (akam) and war (puram)
Pali Literature: Tripitaka (Buddhist canon) — Vinaya Pitaka, Sutta Pitaka, Abhidhamma Pitaka
Prakrit Literature: Jain texts; Agamas
Kalidasa (4th–5th CE): Sanskrit's greatest poet and playwright; Meghadutam, Raghuvamsham, Kumarasambhavam, Shakuntalam, Vikramorvaishiyam, Malavikagnimitram
Banabhatta (7th CE): Harshacharita (Harsha's biography), Kadambari (first novel in Sanskrit)
Medieval vernacular literature: Kabir (dohas), Tulsidas (Ramcharitmanas — Awadhi), Surdas (Sursagar — Braj Bhasha), Mirabai (Padas — Rajasthani/Braj)
Amir Khusro (1253–1325): 'Parrot of India'; invented khayal, tarana, qawwali; introduced sitar and tabla; wrote in Persian and Hindawi; disciple of Nizamuddin Auliya
cards.alert: ⭐ Mahabharata: 18 parvas (books) + Harivamsa; 'What is here is found elsewhere, what is not here is nowhere'. Ramayana: 7 kandas. Both classified as Itihasa (history/epic), not Purana. 18 Puranas — Bhagavata Purana most popular.

cards.heading: UNESCO World Heritage Sites & GI Tags
cards.alert: ⭐ India has 43 UNESCO World Heritage Sites (2024): 35 Cultural + 7 Natural + 1 Mixed. Moidams (Ahom mound-burial sites) is the latest addition (2024). GI Tag: Darjeeling Tea (first GI tag in India 2004), Basmati Rice, Alphonso Mango, Kanchipuram Silk, Kolhapuri Chappal.
